title: Publishable benchmark (n=60, Sonnet+Haiku, 3 invariant classes): architectural invariant in context cuts violations 67%→13% overall, 100%→0% on every tempted cell incl. security must-reach on both models
context: Built the repeatable harness (workflow arch-conformance-benchmark; spec in bench/architectural-conformance.md): 3 invariant classes × 2 models × on/off × 5 samples = 60 deterministically-scored runs. Goal: the citable prevent-rate number for the Architectural Conformance wedge.
decision: Results: OVERALL OFF 67% violate → ON 13% (n=30 each). Per cell: must-reach (charge must call verifySession, security) Sonnet 100→0, Haiku 100→0; layering (controller↛DB) Sonnet 100→0, Haiku 100→80; dep-direction (domain↛express) Sonnet 0/0, Haiku 0/0.
consequences: Headline, stated honestly: where a capable model was genuinely tempted, the recorded invariant flips the violation to 0% — all Sonnet-tempted cells + Haiku/must-reach went 100%→0%. The SECURITY invariant (must-reach verifySession) is the most robust: 100%→0% on BOTH models. Every OFF violation passes a linter clean (the architectural class SAST can't see).

Honest caveats baked into the published number: (a) weaker model heeds less — Haiku ignored the LAYERING rule 4/5 (ON 80%), which is exactly why the deterministic CATCH gate (model-independent) exists; (b) dep-direction was a 0/0 null because the fromRequest(req) task didn't tempt an express import (agents typed req as a plain object) — methodology fix: use a task where the framework type is the reflexive reach. These two no-effect cells drag the 67→13 aggregate; the per-cell table is the real signal.

The wedge now has a citable, reproducible PREVENT number alongside the deterministic CATCH proof (demo + gate + tests). bench/architectural-conformance.md is the published spec; the workflow is the runnable harness.
Next hardening for a stronger headline: re-run dep-direction with a stronger-tempting task, add a third model, raise n/cell.
